flashcards.front: What is a constructor in C++ and what are its key characteristics?
flashcards.back: A constructor is a special member function that automatically executes when an object is created. It has the same name as the class, no return type, and can be overloaded. If no constructor is defined, the compiler provides a default constructor.

flashcards.front: What is a destructor in C++ and when is it invoked?
flashcards.back: A destructor is a special member function that cleans up resources when an object is destroyed. It has the same name as the class prefixed with (~), takes no parameters, and is automatically called when an object goes out of scope or is explicitly deleted.

flashcards.front: Explain the three main types of constructors in C++ with examples.
flashcards.back: Default constructor: takes no arguments (e.g., MyClass()). Parameterized constructor: accepts arguments (e.g., MyClass(int x)). Copy constructor: creates object from existing object (e.g., MyClass(const MyClass& obj)). Move constructor transfers ownership of resources.

flashcards.front: What is an initializer list and why is it preferred over assignment in constructors?
flashcards.back: An initializer list initializes members directly before the constructor body executes (e.g., MyClass(): x(0), y(0) {}). It is required for const members, reference members, and base classes, and avoids unnecessary default construction followed by assignment, improving performance.

flashcards.front: Why are virtual destructors essential in C++ polymorphism?
flashcards.back: Virtual destructors ensure proper cleanup when a derived class object is deleted through a base class pointer. Without a virtual destructor, only the base class destructor runs, causing memory leaks. Always declare destructors virtual in classes intended as base classes.

flashcards.front: What is the copy assignment operator and how does it differ from the copy constructor?
flashcards.back: The copy assignment operator (=) assigns values to an already-existing object from another object. The copy constructor creates and initializes a new object. Syntax: MyClass& operator=(const MyClass& obj). Both perform shallow/deep copying based on implementation.

flashcards.front: What is the order of destructor call in inheritance hierarchy?
flashcards.back: In inheritance, destructors execute in reverse order of construction: derived class destructor runs first, then base class destructor. This ensures derived class resources are cleaned before base class cleanup, maintaining proper encapsulation and resource management.

flashcards.front: What are delegating constructors in C++11 and what is their advantage?
flashcards.back: Delegating constructors allow one constructor to call another constructor of the same class (e.g., MyClass(): MyClass(0,0) {}). This reduces code duplication by centralizing initialization logic, improves maintainability, and ensures consistent initialization across all constructors.
